svg.text: AMERICAN SIGN LANGUAGE
GRAMMAR — SPATIAL SYNTAX
ASL SPATIAL GRAMMAR
ASL exploits 3D signing space for grammar (not possible in spoken language)
Nouns introduced and assigned to specific locations in space
Pointing to those locations = pronouns (no spoken pronouns needed)
Verbs can be directed between locations = subject-object agreement
TOPIC-COMMENT STRUCTURE
ASL word order: Topic-Comment (not SVO/SOV like English)
BOOK [raised brows] WOMAN GIVE = "The book, the woman gave it"
Topic marked by raised eyebrows (NMM); comment follows
Flexibility: word order serves discourse, not syntactic grammar
CLASSIFIERS IN ASL
Classifiers: handshapes that represent categories of referents
Flat hand = vehicle • 2-finger V = person walking • C = cylinder
VEHICLE-CL move-in-arc = "the car drove around the corner"
Classifiers: simultaneous morphology (impossible in linear speech)
LSM GRAMMAR · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
LSM SPATIAL GRAMMAR
LSM uses same spatial grammar as ASL (universal sign language feature)
Loci (locations in space), verb agreement, topic-comment: all present
Spatial grammar appears in ALL sign languages studied worldwide
LSM UNIQUE FEATURES
LSM: some signs influenced by Mexican Spanish mouthing/lipreading
Initialized signs: handshape = first letter of Spanish word (like ASL uses English)
Number signs: differ from ASL (Mexican vs US counting conventions)
WHAT ALL SIGN LANGUAGES SHARE
All sign languages: spatial grammar, classifiers, NMM, iconicity
All sign languages: topic-comment or OSV-biased word order
All sign languages: simultaneous morphology (encode multiple meanings at once)
Signed languages: NOT universal; each community has its own language
PLATE 62c ALT · ASL / LSM · GRAMMAR
